question: What is the literary structure of Revelation 12-14 and how does the placement of the three angels' messages within this macro-structure determine their meaning? What comes before (Rev 12's cosmic conflict, Rev 13's two beasts), what comes after (Rev 14:13-20's harvest), and how does the Lamb-on-Zion unit (14:1-5) frame the messages? How does Rev 11:19 (the ark of the covenant revealed) open the entire sequence? Are the three angels literal angels or symbolic movements — what does the apocalyptic genre require?
prior_research_summary: Seven prior studies provide directly relevant findings: revelation-structure (chiastic center at Rev 12-14), day-of-atonement-revelation-chiasm (DOA liturgical parallel), rev-14-harvest-of-earth (dual-harvest eschatology), rev14-144000-sequence (Lamb vs Son of Man framing), hist-12-revelation-13-14-beast-three-angels (composite beast, hupomone inclusio, fourfold universal formula), three-angels-messages (earlier comprehensive treatment), and 3am-00-series-methodology (phrase mapping, competing-messages framework, Rev 10-14 bridge). External corpus leads from EGW (angels symbolize human proclaimers, ark-to-third-angel connection, Rev 12 war as end-time template), Bohr (sanctuary furniture transition at 11:19, Sinai theophany parallel, Rev 13-15 structural unit), and web research (Strand's chiastic structure, Shea's central chiasm, Beale's seven-unit 4+2+1 pattern, semeion inclusio, kai eidon scene markers).

required_verses.reason: Structural introduction — ark of covenant opened, theophany phenomena; opens the entire cosmic conflict unit
First sign (semeion) — woman, dragon, manchild; sets cosmic conflict stage
War in heaven — Michael defeats dragon; victory through blood of Lamb (12:11); proposed chiastic center
Dragon's pursuit of woman and remnant — 'keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ' (12:17)
Sea beast — composite Daniel 7 entity, mouth speaking great things, 42-month authority, hupomone marker at 13:10
Earth beast — lamb-like horns but dragon speech, makes image, imposes mark (charagma), number 666
Lamb on Mount Zion with 144,000 — contrast-frame to Rev 13; Father's name vs. mark; firstfruits (aparche)
First angel's message — eternal gospel, fear God, give glory, hour of judgment, worship Creator
Second angel's message — Babylon is fallen, wine of fornication
Third angel's message — beast worship warning, mark, wine of wrath, torment
Hupomone conclusion — patience of the saints, commandments of God, faith of Jesus; inclusio with 13:10
Beatitude — blessed are the dead who die in the Lord; transition to harvest
Grain harvest — Son of Man on cloud with sickle; positive harvest paralleling Dan 7:13
Grape harvest — angel from temple, altar-fire angel, winepress of wrath; negative harvest
Victory over the beast scene — possible extension of the literary unit; semeion at 15:1
Temple opened in heaven — structural parallel to 11:19; no one able to enter (probation close parallel)
Second sign (semeion) — great red dragon; part of semeion inclusio with 12:1 and 15:1
stoma laloun megala — verbatim quotation from Dan 7:8 LXX; key intertextual link
Fourfold universal formula (ethnos, phyle, glossa, laos) — beast's dominion claim paralleling 14:6
Angels of the seven churches — key test case for angelos usage in Revelation (literal vs. symbolic)
Angel of Ephesus — church angel as possible human leader/messenger
Son of Man coming with clouds to receive kingdom — OT background for Rev 14:14
Fourth beast with ten horns, little horn speaking great things — source text for Rev 13:1-7
Sinai theophany — thunderings, lightnings, earthquake; parallel to Rev 11:19 phenomena
Harvest and winepress imagery — OT source for Rev 14:14-20 dual harvest
Lucifer's fall — OT background for Rev 12:7-9 war in heaven narrative
Leviathan/dragon imagery — OT dragon tradition behind Rev 12:3-4
Pharaoh as dragon (tannin) — OT background for dragon symbolism in Revelation
Original serpent/woman conflict — protoevangelium behind Rev 12:1-17
Nebuchadnezzar's image worship — OT parallel to Rev 13's image-worship compulsion
Michael standing up — connects to Rev 12:7 Michael war narrative
Mount Zion as heavenly Jerusalem — interpretive key for Rev 14:1 location
Prophesy again — three of four Rev 14:6 audience terms (peoples, nations, tongues); Rev 10-14 bridge
Altar souls — altar vindication arc continuing through 8:5 to 14:18
Censer scene — altar-fire imagery connecting to 14:18 harvest authorization
Dragon, beast, false prophet — counterfeit trinity named explicitly later
False prophet identification — earth beast/false prophet equation confirming Rev 13 symbolism
Dragon identified — 'that old serpent, which is the Devil, and Satan'; confirms Rev 12:9 identification chain
Day of Atonement ark ritual — background for Rev 11:19 ark theology
Ark of the covenant construction — contents (law, manna, rod); significance for Rev 11:19
Dragon/sea monster defeated — OT divine warrior tradition behind Rev 12
Harvest as end of the world — Jesus' harvest eschatology background for Rev 14:14-16
Sealing of 144,000 — earlier sealing scene connecting to Rev 14:1 Father's name on forehead
Creator worship — worship the one who created; parallel to Rev 14:7 Creator-worship call
Babylon's golden cup / nations drunk — OT source for Rev 14:8 Babylon fallen language
Winepress of divine wrath — OT background for Rev 14:19-20 grape treading

focus_areas.title: Rev 11:19 as structural introduction to the cosmic conflict unit
focus_areas.what: Determine whether Rev 11:19 functions as the conclusion of the seventh trumpet section or as the introduction to the Rev 12-14 cosmic conflict unit
focus_areas.why: The structural role of 11:19 determines whether the ark/covenant/commandment theme opens the entire sequence or merely closes the trumpet narrative — multiple scholars (Strand, Shea, Stefanovic) claim it opens forward
focus_areas.how: Parse Rev 11:19 with greek_parser.py, compare theophany phenomena with Exo 19:16-19 Sinai theophany, trace naos (G3485) usage across Revelation, map sanctuary furniture references before and after 11:19
focus_areas.narrative: This is the architectural hinge of the study. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G3485-naos.md` for the naos/hieron distinction — naos specifically means the inner sanctuary, not the temple complex. Run greek_parser.py --verse on Rev 11:19 to get the full grammatical structure. Compare the theophany phenomena (lightnings, voices, thunderings, earthquake, great hail) with Exo 19:16-19 — Bohr and multiple scholars claim these match the Sinai theophany, strengthening the law/covenant connection. Map all sanctuary furniture references in Revelation: before 11:19 (lampstands in 1:12-13, incense altar in 8:3-5) vs. after 11:19 (ark of the covenant). This progression from Holy Place to Most Holy Place furniture supports the Day of Atonement transition claimed by the prior day-of-atonement-revelation-chiasm study. Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py --hybrid-ot and --hybrid-nt on Rev 11:19. Check the grammar reference entry at `$GRAMMAR_REF/greek/apocalyptic-narrative-prolepsis.md` for how kai eidon framing works in apocalyptic narrative.

focus_areas.title: The semeion inclusio and kai eidon structural markers
focus_areas.what: Map all occurrences of semeion (sign) in Rev 12:1, 12:3, and 15:1 and all kai eidon (and I saw) transitions in Rev 12-15 to test the structural boundary claims
focus_areas.why: Beale claims semeion creates an inclusio marking the literary boundaries of the unit, and kai eidon marks scene transitions — these are verifiable textual markers that either confirm or refute the structural claims
focus_areas.how: Search for G4592 semeion occurrences in Revelation, map all kai eidon (G2532+G1492) combinations in Rev 12-15, compare with Beale's seven-unit structure
focus_areas.narrative: READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G4592-semeion.md` for the full range of semeion usage. The claim is that semeion appears at 12:1 ('a great sign in heaven'), 12:3 ('another sign in heaven'), and 15:1 ('another sign in heaven, great and marvellous') — creating an inclusio that brackets the entire cosmic conflict unit. Run search_strongs.py --verses G4592 to get ALL Revelation occurrences. Then trace every kai eidon in chapters 12-15 — each one marks a new scene in Beale's seven-unit structure: (1) 12:1-18 dragon, (2) 13:1-10 sea beast, (3) 13:11-18 earth beast, (4) 14:1-5 Lamb, (5) 14:6-13 three angels, (6) 14:14-20 harvest, (7) 15:2-4 victory. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 12:1, 12:3, 13:1, 13:11, 14:1, 14:6, 14:14, 15:1 to identify the structural transition markers. Check whether the 4+2+1 pattern claimed by Beale holds up textually.

focus_areas.title: Rev 12's cosmic conflict and the dragon identification chain
focus_areas.what: Analyze the structure of Rev 12 as the opening movement of the cosmic conflict: woman-dragon-manchild (12:1-6), war in heaven (12:7-12), pursuit of the remnant (12:13-17)
focus_areas.why: Rev 12 sets the cosmic backstory for everything that follows — the dragon's identity, motive, and method determine the meaning of the beasts and the angels' counter-messages
focus_areas.how: Parse Rev 12:9 for the fourfold identification chain, trace drakon (G1404) through Revelation, examine verb tenses in 12:7-12 for temporal framing
focus_areas.narrative: READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G1404-drakon.md` and `$WORD_STUDIES/G3789-ophis.md`. Rev 12:9 provides a fourfold identification chain: 'the great dragon' (ho drakon ho megas), 'that old serpent' (ho ophis ho archaios), 'called the Devil' (ho kaloumenos Diabolos), 'and Satan' (kai ho Satanas). Run greek_parser.py --verse on Rev 12:7-9 and Rev 12:10-12 to examine verb tenses — EGW claims the war continues through history; the text's temporal markers need verification. Check the grammar reference at `$GRAMMAR_REF/greek/apocalyptic-narrative-prolepsis.md` for the prolepsis/recursion pattern in Rev 12. Rev 12:11 ('they overcame him by the blood of the Lamb') is claimed by Shea to be at the chiastic center of the entire book. Rev 12:17 is critical: the remnant 'keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ' — this language recurs at 14:12, forming a thematic inclusio. Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py on Rev 12:7-9 for OT dragon/serpent background (Isa 14:12-14, Isa 27:1, Psa 74:13, Ezk 29:3).

focus_areas.title: Rev 13's two beasts as counterfeit trinity and competing worship system
focus_areas.what: Analyze the sea beast and earth beast as a structural system: their Daniel 7 composite nature, their claims to worship, and their imposition of the mark (charagma)
focus_areas.why: The beasts of Rev 13 constitute the adversary system that the three angels directly counter — understanding the beasts' structure is necessary to understand why the angels say what they say
focus_areas.how: Parse key verses, trace the fourfold universal formula, map the Daniel 7 intertextual connections, analyze the worship-compulsion vocabulary
focus_areas.narrative: READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2342-therion.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G5480-charagma.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G1504-eikon.md`, and `$WORD_STUDIES/G4352-proskyneo.md`. The sea beast is a composite of all four Daniel 7 beasts (leopard, bear, lion + 7 heads and 10 horns). Run greek_parser.py on Rev 13:1-2 and compare with Dan 7:3-7 LXX. The verbatim quotation stoma laloun megala (Rev 13:5 = Dan 7:8 LXX) needs verification via parser. Rev 13:7 has the fourfold universal formula (ethnos, phyle, glossa, laos) — the SAME formula appears in Rev 14:6, creating a deliberate structural contrast between beast-dominion and angel-proclamation. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G1849-exousia.md` for the 'power/authority' language. The earth beast (13:11-18) looks like a lamb but speaks as a dragon — direct contrast to the true Lamb of 14:1. The mark (charagma) imposed by the earth beast contrasts with the Father's name on the forehead of the 144,000 (14:1). Check the grammar reference at `$GRAMMAR_REF/greek/aorist-passive-indicative.md` and `$GRAMMAR_REF/greek/aorist-infinitive-accusative-of-duration.md` for the edothe (divine passive) pattern in Rev 13:5,7 and the poiesai menas construction. Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py on Rev 13:5 and Dan 7:8.

focus_areas.title: Rev 14:1-5 as deliberate contrast-frame to Rev 13
focus_areas.what: Analyze how the Lamb-on-Zion pericope functions as a structural counter-image to the beast system: seal vs. mark, Mount Zion vs. earth, Lamb vs. beast, 144,000 vs. beast-worshipers
focus_areas.why: The Lamb-on-Zion unit is the immediate literary context that frames the three angels' messages — the messages emerge from a position of victory (Lamb already with His people) not desperation
focus_areas.how: Parse Rev 14:1-5, compare vocabulary with Rev 13, trace aparche (firstfruits) connection to the harvest, analyze the heavenly Mount Zion identification
focus_areas.narrative: READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G721-arnion.md` and `$WORD_STUDIES/G536-aparche.md`. The prior study rev14-144000-sequence established that Mount Zion = heavenly Jerusalem (Heb 12:22-23), supported by 14:3 ('before the throne') and 14:5 ('before the throne of God'). Run greek_parser.py on Rev 14:1-5. The contrast structure is point-by-point: (1) Lamb (arnion) vs. beast (therion) — near-homophone; (2) Father's name (onoma) on forehead vs. mark (charagma) on forehead/hand; (3) singing a new song vs. compelled worship; (4) 'not defiled with women' = holy war consecration vs. Babylon's fornication; (5) firstfruits (aparche) = guarantee of the coming harvest (14:14-16). The 144,000 as aparche directly connects to the grain harvest — they are the representative sample guaranteeing the full harvest. This framing means the three angels speak from an already-secured victory, not an uncertain outcome. Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py on Rev 14:1 for OT Zion traditions.

focus_areas.title: The three angels' messages as structural counter-proclamation to the beasts
focus_areas.what: Analyze the content and structure of Rev 14:6-12 as a systematic response to Rev 13's beast system, including the shared vocabulary and the hupomone inclusio
focus_areas.why: The placement of the three messages between the Lamb scene (14:1-5) and the harvest (14:14-20) is deliberately structural — they are the last call before judgment executes
focus_areas.how: Parse Rev 14:6-12, map shared vocabulary between Rev 13 and Rev 14, verify the hupomone inclusio (13:10 to 14:12), trace each message's content back to what it counters in Rev 13
focus_areas.narrative: READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2098-euangelion.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G166-aionios.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G5399-phobeo.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G1391-doxa.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G2919-krino.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G4352-proskyneo.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G897-babylon.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G2372-thumos.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G5281-hupomone.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G1785-entole.md`, and `$WORD_STUDIES/G4102-pistis.md`. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 14:6-7, 14:8, 14:9-11, and 14:12 separately. The first angel counters the beast's worship claim with 'worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea' — this creation language echoes Exo 20:11 (Sabbath commandment). The second angel announces Babylon's fall using Jer 51:7-8 language. The third angel warns against the very worship system Rev 13 establishes. Rev 14:12 closes with the hupomone formula — 'here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.' Compare this with Rev 13:10: 'here is the patience and the faith of the saints.' These two verses bracket the entire three-angels section. The fourfold audience formula at 14:6 (ethnos, phyle, glossa, laos) matches Rev 13:7 — the angel's proclamation reaches the same universal scope as the beast's dominion. Check the grammar reference at `$GRAMMAR_REF/greek/participle-legon-discourse.md` for the legon participle structure in Rev 14.

focus_areas.title: Rev 14:13-20 dual harvest as climactic conclusion
focus_areas.what: Analyze the grain harvest (14:14-16) and grape harvest (14:17-20) as the eschatological conclusion of the literary unit opened by 11:19
focus_areas.why: The dual harvest is not merely an appendix but the structural climax — the entire 11:19-14:20 unit moves toward this judgment/salvation bifurcation
focus_areas.how: Parse Rev 14:14-20, trace Son of Man/cloud language to Dan 7:13, identify Joel 3:13 as OT source, analyze the therizo/trygao lexical distinction
focus_areas.narrative: READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2325-therizo.md` and `$WORD_STUDIES/G2372-thumos.md`. The prior study rev-14-harvest-of-earth established five independent indicators distinguishing the grain and grape harvests: therizo/trygao lexical distinction, Son-of-Man/angel agent distinction, absent/present wrath-language, silent/catastrophic outcome distinction, firstfruits-guarantee chain. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 14:14, 14:15-16, 14:17-18, and 14:19-20. The Son of Man sitting on the cloud (14:14) uses language from Dan 7:13 with a preposition shift (META to EPI — approaching to enthroned). The angel from the altar (14:18) continues the altar-fire vindication arc from Rev 6:9 through 8:5. Joel 3:13 is the OT bifurcation source: 'Put ye in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe: come, get you down; for the press is full, the fats overflow.' John splits Joel's unified image into two morally distinct operations. The thumos (wrath) chain extends from 14:8 through 14:10, 14:19, 15:1, 16:1, to 19:15. Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py on Rev 14:14 and Joel 3:13.

focus_areas.title: Literal vs. symbolic angels — what does the apocalyptic genre require?
focus_areas.what: Determine whether the three angels of Rev 14:6-12 are literal celestial beings or symbolic representations of human proclamation movements, based on genre analysis and internal Revelation evidence
focus_areas.why: This is explicitly assigned to this study by the 3am-00 methodology study (Objection #11). The answer determines whether the messages are delivered by God directly or through human agency
focus_areas.how: Map angelos (G32) usage throughout Revelation, analyze the seven church angels (Rev 1:20, 2:1 etc.), compare beast symbolism with angel symbolism for structural consistency, check apocalyptic genre conventions
focus_areas.narrative: The question is NOT whether angelos CAN mean human messenger (it can — G32 covers both celestial and human messengers). The question is whether the apocalyptic genre's symbolic world requires symbolic figures. Run search_strongs.py --verses G32 to map ALL angelos occurrences in Revelation — there are approximately 67. Categorize them: (a) clearly celestial (Rev 7:1, 8:2, etc.), (b) clearly symbolic/human (Rev 2:1 angel of the church?), (c) ambiguous. The strongest structural argument: the beasts of Rev 13 are universally understood as symbolic of earthly powers/systems, not literal animals. If the antagonists are symbolic, consistency suggests the protagonists (angels) are as well. EGW explicitly states the three angels 'symbolize the work of those who proclaim' the messages (6T 17.4). The gospel content of the first angel's message echoes Great Commission language given to human agents (Mat 28:19-20, Mark 16:15). However, the internal vision-world consistently presents angels as celestial figures who act. The resolution likely involves distinguishing the vision-world depiction (heavenly figures) from the real-world referent (human proclamation). Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py on Rev 14:6 to check for Great Commission language parallels.

focus_areas.title: The chiastic structure and competing-messages framework
focus_areas.what: Evaluate the chiastic arrangement of Rev 11:19-15:4 (or 12:1-14:20) as proposed by Strand, Shea, and Beale, and verify the competing-messages framework (dragon/beasts vs. three angels)
focus_areas.why: The chiastic position determines emphasis — if Rev 12-14 is the center of Revelation's macro-chiasm, the three angels' messages carry maximum structural weight. The competing-messages framework shapes interpretation of every element
focus_areas.how: Map the proposed chiastic pairs, test vocabulary links between corresponding sections, verify the prior revelation-structure study's placement
focus_areas.narrative: The prior revelation-structure study established Rev 12-14 as section E — the chiastic center of Revelation's structure: A (Prologue) ↔ A' (Epilogue), B (Churches) ↔ B' (New Jerusalem), C (Seals) ↔ C' (Judgment Executed), D (Trumpets) ↔ D' (Bowls), E (THE CENTER: 12:1-14:20). Shea further argues the CENTER has its own internal chiasm with 12:10-11 and 14:14 as the pivot. The competing-messages framework identifies structural pairs: dragon's deception (12:9) vs. angel's truth (14:6-7); beast's worship compulsion (13:12-15) vs. angel's worship call (14:7); mark of the beast (13:16-17) vs. seal of God (7:3-4, 14:1); Babylon's wine (14:8) vs. God's wrath-wine (14:10). Map all shared vocabulary between Rev 13 and Rev 14 — particularly proskyneo, charagma, ethnos/phyle/glossa/laos, hupomone, entole. The 'no rest day nor night' of 14:11 inverts the 'rest not day and night' of the living creatures worshiping God (4:8). Verify whether these vocabulary links constitute genuine structural pairing or forced parallelism.

focus_areas.title: The ark/covenant/commandment thematic thread from 11:19 through 14:12
focus_areas.what: Trace the covenant-commandment vocabulary chain from the ark's appearance in 11:19 through the dragon's war against commandment-keepers (12:17) to the saints' endurance formula (14:12)
focus_areas.why: EGW and Bohr both claim that the ark in 11:19 directly connects to the commandments theme in the three angels' messages — this is a verifiable vocabulary chain
focus_areas.how: Map diatheke (G1242) and entole (G1785) occurrences in Rev 11-14, check for nomos usage, trace the 'testimony' language
focus_areas.narrative: READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G1242-diatheke.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G1785-entole.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/H1285-beriyth.md`, and `$WORD_STUDIES/H5715-eduwth.md`. In Rev 11:19, the 'ark of his testament (diatheke)' is seen. The ark contained the Ten Commandments (Exo 25:16,21; Deut 10:5; 1 Ki 8:9). In Rev 12:17, the dragon wages war against those who 'keep the commandments (entole) of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ.' In Rev 14:12, the saints are those who 'keep the commandments (entole) of God, and the faith of Jesus.' This creates a thematic arc: ark revealed (covenant standard) → dragon attacks commandment-keepers → angels call for worship of the Creator → saints keep commandments. The question is whether this is a deliberate literary thread or coincidental vocabulary. Run search_strongs.py --verses G1242 and G1785 in Revelation to map the full distribution. Compare the Sinai theophany phenomena at 11:19 (lightnings, thunderings, earthquake) with the law-giving at Sinai (Exo 19:16-19) — the parallel suggests the ark's revelation is a re-presentation of the law's authority.

required_word_studies.reason: Core to the literal vs. symbolic angels question — messenger can be celestial or human
Dragon symbolism in Rev 12-13; identification chain in 12:9
Beast — both sea beast and earth beast of Rev 13; counterpart to arnion
Lamb — the true Lamb of 14:1 contrasted with beast and dragon; Lamb-beast near-homophone
Sign — structural inclusio markers at 12:1, 12:3, and 15:1
Mark of the beast — contrasted with seal/name of God on 144,000 foreheads
Worship — appears in both Rev 13 (beast worship) and Rev 14 (Creator worship); competing worship systems
Gospel — 'everlasting gospel' of Rev 14:6; first use of euangelion in Revelation
Eternal/everlasting — modifies euangelion in 14:6; important for 'eternal gospel' theology
Patience/endurance — hupomone inclusio at Rev 13:10 and 14:12 bracketing the angels
Commandment — Rev 12:17 and 14:12; connects to ark/covenant theme from 11:19
Temple/sanctuary — inner temple opened at 11:19 and 15:5; sanctuary theology
Wrath — wine of wrath chain through 14:8, 14:10, 14:19, 15:1, 16:1, 19:15
Reap/harvest — grain harvest vocabulary in 14:15-16; distinct from trygao (grape)
Covenant/testament — ark of his testament (diatheke) in 11:19; covenant-commandment thread
Firstfruits — 144,000 as firstfruits (14:4) connecting to grain harvest (14:14-16)
Babylon is fallen — second angel's message (14:8); OT Babylon background
Image — image of the beast (13:14-15); parallel to Dan 3 image worship

required_parsing.reason: Structural pivot — theophany formula, naos and diatheke usage
War in heaven — verb tenses for temporal framing, identification chain in 12:9
Remnant formula — entole and martyria usage, who makes war
Composite beast description — Daniel 7 allusion verification
stoma laloun megala quotation, edothe divine passive, fourfold formula
Lamb on Zion — arnion, oros Sion, onoma on foreheads
First angel — euangelion aionion, phobeo/doxa/krino/proskyneo chain
Second angel — pipto (fell), Babylon, oinos thumos porneia
Third angel — proskyneo/charagma warning, wrath vocabulary
Hupomone formula — comparison with 13:10, entole and pistis
Son of Man on cloud — Dan 7:13 allusion, preposition (epi) usage
Angels of the churches — angelos usage as possible human leaders

required_parallels.reason: OT theophany parallels (Sinai), NT temple parallels
OT dragon/fall traditions (Isa 14, Ezk 28), NT Satan defeat passages
Great Commission language parallels (Mat 28:19, Mark 16:15), OT gospel prophecies
Dan 7:13 Son of Man parallels, OT cloud theophany

grammar_references.description: Apocalyptic narrative prolepsis / visionary recursion — kai eidon framing; Rev 12:1-17, 13:1-10 paradigm
Present participle legon as discourse connector — speech-introducing participle; Rev 14 parallel structure
Perfect passive participle — state from completed action; Rev 5:6 / Rev 13:3 esphagmenon (slain state)
Aorist passive indicative — edothe divine passive in Rev 13:5,7; bounded authorization pattern
Aorist infinitive + accusative of duration — poiesai menas in Rev 13:5
Divine passive (passivum divinum) — God as hidden agent; Revelation edothe pattern
Self-deification speech formulas — Isa 14, Ezk 28, Rev 18:7 parallels

missing_library_entries.reason: Core to literal vs. symbolic angels question — no canonical entry exists for the most frequently used word in Revelation
Grape harvest verb — distinct from therizo; needed for dual-harvest analysis in Rev 14:17-20
Sickle — harvest tool in Rev 14:14-19; connects to Joel 3:13 and Mark 4:29
Winepress — Rev 14:19-20 great winepress of the wrath of God; OT background in Isa 63:2-3

topics_discovered.name: WORSHIP
topics_discovered.subtopic: TO BE RENDERED ONLY TO GOD

external_corpus_leads.claim: The opening of God's temple revealing the ark directly produces the third angel's message — those who accept the law's authority are 'brought to view' in the proclamation
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by mapping the diatheke/entole vocabulary chain from Rev 11:19 through 12:17 to 14:12. Does the text sustain a covenant-law thematic thread?

external_corpus_leads.claim: The three angels 'symbolize the work of those who proclaim the first, second, and third angels' messages' — angels represent human proclaimers, not literal celestial beings
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by checking how angelos (G32) is used throughout Revelation, particularly in Rev 1:20 and 2:1-3:14. If church 'angels' are human leaders, this strengthens the symbolic reading for 14:6-12.

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 12's war in heaven is not just past history but continues into the last days — 'Satan will carry on the great conflict that he began in heaven'
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by examining verb tenses and temporal markers in Rev 12:7-17. Does the text present the conflict as a single past event or an ongoing/recurring pattern?

external_corpus_leads.claim: Before Rev 11:19, Revelation focuses on Holy Place furniture (lampstands, incense altar); 11:19 shifts attention to Most Holy Place furniture (ark) — sanctuary transition
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by mapping ALL sanctuary furniture references in Revelation: chapters 1-11 vs. 12-22. Does the progression match Holy Place → Most Holy Place?

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 11:19's phenomena (thunder, lightning, earthquake, hail) match the Sinai theophany — strengthening the law/covenant connection
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by comparing Rev 11:19 phenomena list with Exo 19:16-19 phenomena list. Do they match exactly?

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 13 begins with pagan Rome bequeathing its throne to papal Rome — structural unit from 11:19 forward, not from chapter 13 alone
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify structurally: does 11:19 function as an introduction to what follows (12:1ff) or as a conclusion to the seventh trumpet (11:15-18)?

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 11:19 is a 'victorious-introduction scene' that opens the cosmic conflict vision — part of an eight-part chiastic structure
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by checking whether 11:19's vocabulary and themes connect forward to 12:1ff or backward to 11:15-18. Run parallels on 11:19.

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 12:10-11 and 14:14 form the chiastic center of the entire book — 'the Christocentric revelation of the inauguration, appropriation, and consummation of salvation through the blood of the Lamb'
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by examining whether 12:10-11 and 14:14 share genuine thematic correspondence. Does 'the blood of the Lamb' (12:11) truly parallel 'the Son of Man on the cloud' (14:14)?

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 11:19-15:4 follows a seven-fold 4+2+1 pattern matching seals/trumpets/bowls, with semeion inclusio (12:1, 12:3, 15:1) and kai eidon markers
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by mapping ALL kai eidon occurrences in Rev 12-15 and all semeion occurrences. Does the 4+2+1 pattern hold?

external_corpus_leads.claim: There are 'two sets of competing messages' — dragon/beasts vs. three angels — with deliberate structural pairing in vocabulary and form
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by mapping shared vocabulary: proskyneo, charagma, ethnos/phyle/glossa/laos, hupomone, entole across Rev 13-14.

research_instructions:
This study requires careful attention to structural and literary features rather than just word-level analysis. The research agent should:

1. **Map ALL kai eidon (and I saw) occurrences** in Rev 12-15 — these are claimed to be scene-transition markers. List verse reference, what follows, and whether it introduces a new vision unit.

2. **Map ALL semeion (sign) occurrences** in Revelation — test the inclusio claim (12:1, 12:3, 15:1).

3. **Map ALL proskyneo (worship) occurrences** in Rev 13-14 — both beast-worship and God-worship instances. Note who worships whom and under what conditions.

4. **Map the fourfold universal formula** (ethnos, phyle, glossa, laos) appearances in Revelation — at minimum Rev 5:9, 7:9, 10:11, 11:9, 13:7, 14:6, 17:15.

5. **Compare Rev 13:10 with Rev 14:12 verbatim** — the hupomone inclusio claim needs exact Greek text comparison.

6. **Map sanctuary furniture references** across all of Revelation — list what is mentioned, where, and whether it is Holy Place or Most Holy Place furniture. This tests Bohr's furniture-transition claim.

7. **Count angelos occurrences** in Revelation by category: clearly celestial, possibly human (church angels), and the three angels of Rev 14.

8. **Verify Dan 7:8 LXX quotation** in Rev 13:5 — run parser on both verses and compare exact Greek.

9. All external corpus claims should be verified against the biblical text, not assumed true. Each claim in external_corpus_leads has a specific verification directive — follow it.
